teachingThesis: For efficient data analysis, databases are often structured into schemas like the Star and Snowflake models; the Star Schema prioritizes fast queries with a simple, denormalized design, while the Snowflake Schema saves space through normalization at the cost of more complex, slower queries.
learningGoals: Define the core components of a data warehouse schema: fact, measure, dimension, fact table, and dimension table.
Explain the structure and characteristics of a Star Schema.
Interpret a Star Schema diagram to identify its fact table, dimension tables, keys, and measures.
Explain how a Snowflake Schema differs from a Star Schema, focusing on normalized dimensions.
Interpret a Snowflake Schema diagram, identifying the hierarchical relationships between dimension tables.
Compare the Star and Snowflake schemas, articulating the trade-offs between query performance, disk space, and maintenance complexity.
prerequisites: Basic understanding of SQL and database concepts like tables, columns, primary keys, and foreign keys.
Familiarity with the concept of database normalization.
Knowledge of how SQL JOINs work.

mustUseSourceRefs: Definition of Measure/fact, Fact table, Dimension, and Dimension table
Definition of Snowflake Schema as having 'Normalized dimension tables'
Characteristics of Snowflake Schema list (pros and cons)

externalSearchQueries: star schema vs snowflake schema explained
when to use star schema vs snowflake schema
fact table vs dimension table examples

segmentOutline.title: The Language of Data Warehousing: Facts & Dimensions
segmentOutline.learningGoal: Define fact, measure, dimension, fact table, and dimension table.
segmentOutline.focus: Establishing the fundamental vocabulary required to understand data warehouse schemas, using the definitions and examples provided.
mustCover: Definition of Measure/fact with 'selling price' example.
Definition of Fact table with 'sale table' example and its role in aggregation.
Definition of Dimension with 'product' example.
Definition of Dimension table with 'product table' example.

segmentOutline.title: The Star Schema: A Simple & Fast Model
segmentOutline.learningGoal: Explain the structure and characteristics of a Star Schema.
segmentOutline.focus: Defining the Star Schema and its rules, such as the central fact table, direct joins from dimensions, and the denormalized nature of its dimension tables.
mustCover: Core concept: a central fact table with associated dimension tables.
Characteristic: One table per dimension.
Key rule: Dimension tables are joined to the fact table but not to each other.
Content of fact tables: keys and measures.
Content of dimension tables: attributes.
Note on normalization: Dimension tables may not be normalized.

segmentOutline.title: Anatomy of a Star Schema: A Visual Walkthrough
segmentOutline.learningGoal: Interpret a Star Schema diagram to identify its fact table, dimension tables, keys, and measures.
segmentOutline.focus: Applying the rules of the Star Schema to the provided diagram, tracing the relationships between the central fact table and its surrounding dimension tables.
mustCover: Identify 'Sale' as the central fact table.
Identify 'Price' as a measure within the fact table.
Identify the various 'Id' columns in the 'Sale' table as foreign keys.
Identify 'Customer', 'Product', 'Invoice', etc., as dimension tables.
Visually confirm that all dimension tables connect directly to the 'Sale' table and not to each other.

segmentOutline.title: The Snowflake Schema: Normalizing the Dimensions
segmentOutline.learningGoal: Explain how a Snowflake Schema differs from a Star Schema, focusing on normalized dimensions.
segmentOutline.focus: Defining the Snowflake Schema by its core difference from the Star Schema—the normalization of dimension tables—and discussing the resulting pros and cons.
mustCover: Core concept: 'Normalized dimension tables'.
Benefit: Uses smaller disk space.
Benefit: Easier to implement a new dimension.
Drawback: Reduced query performance due to multiple tables/joins.
Drawback: More maintenance efforts due to more lookup tables.

segmentOutline.title: Anatomy of a Snowflake Schema: A Visual Walkthrough
segmentOutline.learningGoal: Interpret a Snowflake Schema diagram, identifying the hierarchical relationships between dimension tables.
segmentOutline.focus: Visually analyzing the Snowflake diagram to highlight the normalized, hierarchical structure of the dimensions, contrasting it with the flat structure of the Star Schema.
mustCover: Identify 'Sale' as the central fact table.
Trace the normalized dimension 'Product' to its sub-dimension 'Category'.
Trace the normalized dimension 'Invoice' to its sub-dimension 'Outlet'.
Explain that this hierarchical structure is what defines the Snowflake schema and leads to more joins.

segmentOutline.title: Star vs. Snowflake: The Great Trade-off
segmentOutline.learningGoal: Compare the Star and Snowflake schemas, articulating the trade-offs between query performance, disk space, and maintenance complexity.
segmentOutline.focus: Synthesizing information from both schemas to create a direct comparison, helping students understand the decision-making process when choosing a schema.
mustCover: Query Performance: Star is faster due to fewer joins.
Storage Space: Snowflake is more efficient due to less data redundancy.
Schema Complexity: Star is simpler with a flat structure.
Data Integrity/Maintenance: Snowflake can be easier to maintain due to normalization, but the higher number of tables adds complexity.
Use Case Summary: Star for performance-critical data marts; Snowflake for centralized data warehouses where space and data consistency are priorities.
